records.ety: [OE. fader, AS. fæder; akin to OS. fadar, D. vader, OHG. fatar, G. vater, Icel. faðir Sw. & Dan. fader, OIr. athir, L. pater, Gr. path`r, Skr. pitr, perh. fr. Skr. pā protect. √75, 247. Cf. Papa, Paternal, Patriot, Potential, Pablum.]

defs: One who has begotten a child, whether son or daughter; a generator; a male parent.
quotes: A wise son maketh a glad father.

defs: A male ancestor more remote than a parent; a progenitor; especially, a first ancestor; a founder of a race or family; -- in the plural, fathers, ancestors.
quotes: Abraham, who is the father of us all.

defs: One who performs the offices of a parent by maintenance, affetionate care, counsel, or protection.
quotes: I was a father to the poor.
He hath made me a father to Pharaoh, and lord of all his house.

defs: A respectful mode of address to an old man.
quotes: And Joash the king of Israel came down unto him [Elisha], . . . and said, O my father, my father!

defs: A dignitary of the church, a superior of a convent, a confessor (called also father confessor), or a priest; also, the eldest member of a profession, or of a legislative assembly, etc.
One of the chief ecclesiastical authorities of the first centuries after Christ; -- often spoken of collectively as the Fathers; as, the Latin, Greek, or apostolic Fathers.
One who, or that which, gives origin; an originator; a producer, author, or contriver; the first to practice any art, profession, or occupation; a distinguished example or teacher.
quotes: The father of all such as handle the harp and organ.
Might be the father, Harry, to that thought.

defs: The Supreme Being and Creator; God; in theology, the first person in the Trinity.
quotes: Our Father, which art in heaven.
Now had the almighty Father from above . . . Bent down his eye.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Fathered (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Fathering.]

defs: To make one's self the father of; to beget.
quotes: Cowards father cowards, and base things sire base.

defs: To take as one's own child; to adopt; hence, to assume as one's own work; to acknowledge one's self author of or responsible for (a statement, policy, etc.).
quotes: Men of wit Often fathered what he writ.
Think you I am no stronger than my sex, Being so fathered and so husbanded ?
